description: The figure image was not provided or is unreadable. Based on the accompanying text extraction summary, this study investigates dynamic recrystallization (DRX) behavior of low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel (nominal composition: Fe-25.14Mn-10Al-1.46C-0.053Nb wt%) under hot deformation conditions. The experimental data encompasses isothermal constant strain rate compression tests conducted at temperatures ranging from 900°C to 1150°C with strain rates of 0.01–10 s⁻¹. The figure likely presents true stress-true strain curves exhibiting typical DRX characteristics with distinct peak stresses, or possibly the DRX state diagram correlating deformation parameters with grain size evolution. The text reports peak stresses decreasing from 305.4 MPa at 900°C/0.01 s⁻¹ to 53.8 MPa at 1150°C/0.01 s⁻¹, and a calculated DRX activation energy of 669.844 kJ/mol. Specific curve shapes, axis scales, or microstructural features cannot be visually verified due to the missing image.

This optical micrograph displays the initial as-received microstructure of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density high-strength steel (FeeMneAleC steel) prior to hot deformation testing. The image reveals a polycrystalline austenitic structure with clearly delineated grain boundaries appearing as bright white lines against the darker matrix. Within individual grains, parallel striations characteristic of annealing twins are visible, confirming the face-centered cubic (FCC) austenite phase typical of this alloy system. The scale bar indicates 100 μm. According to the caption and experimental description, this micrograph was captured using a LEXT OLS4000 laser confocal microscope after etching with 30% nitric acid alcohol solution (volume fraction). The material composition is 1.46 wt.% C, 25.14 wt.% Mn, 10 wt.% Al, and 0.053 wt.% Nb. This baseline microstructure serves as the reference state for subsequent isothermal constant strain rate compression experiments conducted at temperatures ranging from 900°C to 1150°C and strain rates from 0.01 to 10 s^{-1} using a Gleeble-3500 thermo-mechanical simulator. The initial grain structure exhibits equiaxed austenite grains with heterogeneous sizes and numerous annealing twins, providing the starting condition for studying dynamic recrystallization (DRX) behavior and grain size evolution during hot working.

This figure presents a temperature-time profile schematic illustrating the isothermal constant strain rate compression experimental procedure for FeeMneAleC (Fe-Mn-Al-C) steel conducted on a Gleeble-3500 thermo-mechanical simulator. The thermal cycle comprises heating to 1200°C at 10°C/s, homogenization for 120 s, cooling at 5°C/s to the target deformation temperature (ranging from 900°C to 1150°C per the experimental matrix), a 15 s holding period to ensure thermal equilibrium, followed by compression at constant strain rates (0.01–10 s⁻¹) with 60% reduction (true strain 0.92), and final water quenching. The diagram explicitly labels the heating rate, homogenization conditions, cooling rate, holding time, compression stage, and quenching step, providing a complete thermal processing route for investigating the dynamic recrystallization behavior and microstructure evolution of the low-density high-strength steel under hot deformation conditions.

This figure presents the hot deformation behavior of Fe-Mn-Al-C (FeeMneAleC) low-density high-strength steel through true stress-true strain curves and 3D peak stress surfaces. Subfigures (a) and (b) display flow curves obtained from isothermal constant strain rate compression tests. Figure (a) shows curves at a fixed strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹ across temperatures from 900°C to 1150°C, where peak stress decreases from approximately 305 MPa to 54 MPa with increasing temperature, indicating negative temperature sensitivity. Figure (b) presents curves at a fixed temperature of 1100°C across strain rates from 0.01 to 10 s⁻¹, demonstrating positive strain rate sensitivity as peak stress increases with higher strain rates. All curves exhibit characteristic dynamic recrystallization (DRX) behavior with distinct stress peaks followed by flow softening. Subfigures (c) and (d) provide three-dimensional surface plots illustrating the combined effects of temperature and strain rate on peak stress for strain rate ranges of 0.01–0.1 s⁻¹ and 1–10 s⁻¹, respectively. These results establish the rheological data foundation for constructing DRX state diagrams and grain size prediction models, revealing that the steel is suitable for hot working in windows such as 950–1000°C/0.01–0.1 s⁻¹ for fine grain microstructures.

This figure presents two linearized Arrhenius-type plots used to determine the dynamic recrystallization (DRX) activation energy of low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel subjected to hot compression. Subplot (a) plots the natural logarithm of strain rate ln(ε̇) against ln[sinh(ασp)] for deformation temperatures ranging from 900°C to 1150°C (black: 900°C, red: 950°C, blue: 1000°C, magenta: 1050°C, green: 1100°C, dark blue: 1150°C), yielding an average slope k₁ = 5.135. Subplot (b) plots ln[sinh(ασp)] versus the reciprocal temperature 1000/T (K⁻¹) for strain rates of 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 10 s⁻¹, with an average slope k₂ = 15.690. According to the caption and citation text, these linear fittings were used to calculate the DRX activation energy as 669.844 kJ mol⁻¹. The observed lack of perfect parallelism among the fitted lines suggests that the deformation conditions significantly influence the activation energy, indicating that the calculated value represents an average within the experimental range.

The figure presents a linear regression analysis of normalized stress versus normalized strain data for a low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel (Fe-25.14Mn-10Al-1.46C-0.053Nb wt%) subjected to isothermal compression at 1050°C and a strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹. The plot displays discrete data points (marked with crosses) representing the relationship between ln(σ/σₚ) on the vertical axis and [ln(ε/εₚ) + 1 - ε/εₚ] on the horizontal axis, where σₚ and εₚ denote peak stress and peak strain, respectively. A red solid line indicates the linear fit to these data points, yielding a material constant C of 0.1461 with a correlation coefficient R of 0.9728. This analysis follows the Cingara-McQueen method to determine the critical strain for dynamic recrystallization (DRX). The strong linear correlation validates the constitutive model and enables calculation of DRX parameters, supporting the construction of DRX state diagrams and grain size prediction models for this lightweight steel.

Figure 6 presents the quantitative relationships between the characteristic values of rheological stress curves and the Zener-Hollomon parameter (lnZ) for a Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density high-strength steel, establishing empirical models for dynamic recrystallization (DRX) behavior. The figure comprises three dual-axis scatter plots with linear regression fittings: subplot (a) displays the critical stress σ_c (stars, left axis, R=0.947) and critical strain ε_c (circles, right axis, R=0.911) versus lnZ; subplot (b) shows the peak stress σ_p (stars, left axis, R=0.957) and peak strain ε_p (circles, right axis, R=0.911) versus lnZ; subplot (c) illustrates the steady-state stress σ_s (stars, left axis, R=0.956) and steady-state strain ε_s (circles, right axis, R=0.922) versus lnZ. In all subplots, stress values (in MPa) increase monotonically with lnZ on the left ordinate, while corresponding strain values increase on the right ordinate, with correlation coefficients exceeding 0.91 indicating strong linear dependencies. These relationships, obtained through multiple linear regression as stated in the caption, provide the mathematical foundation for predicting DRX characteristic stresses and strains as functions of the temperature-compensated strain rate parameter (Z), supporting the constitutive modeling of hot deformation behavior in this austenitic steel.

The figure presents a dynamic recrystallization (DRX) state diagram for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density high-strength steel under hot deformation conditions, plotting the natural logarithm of the Zener-Hollomon parameter (lnZ) on the vertical axis against true strain (ε) on the horizontal axis. The diagram features two distinct datasets: open circles represent the critical strain (εc) marking the onset of DRX, while red stars denote the steady-state strain (εs) corresponding to complete DRX. The plot is partitioned into three characteristic deformation regimes: Area A (work hardening zone), Area B (partial DRX zone), and Area C (complete DRX zone). The data demonstrates that both εc and εs increase monotonically with increasing Z parameter (associated with decreasing temperature or increasing strain rate), indicating that DRX initiation and completion become progressively more difficult under higher lnZ conditions. This state diagram establishes quantitative boundaries for processing window selection, confirming that higher deformation resistance (higher Z) requires greater accumulated strain to achieve complete dynamic recrystallization.

This schematic diagram illustrates the distribution of different deformation zones within a cylindrical compression sample of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density high-strength steel after hot deformation. The figure presents a cross-sectional view parallel to the loading direction (indicated by loading force P), revealing three distinct zones marked as 1, 2, and 3. Zone 1 (difficult deformation zone) appears at the top and bottom center regions near the contact surfaces with the compression platens; Zone 2 (small deformation zone) is located at the peripheral edges; and Zone 3 (large deformation zone) occupies the central and transitional areas. The non-uniform deformation pattern arises from friction between the sample contact surfaces and the compression platens, leading to uneven stress distribution during isothermal constant strain rate compression (conducted at 900–1150°C with 60% reduction). This schematic establishes the spatial framework for subsequent microstructural characterization (referenced as Fig. 9 in the source text), where the dynamic recrystallization (DRX) behavior varies significantly across these zones—Zone 3 exhibiting the most substantial DRX while Zone 1 shows minimal deformation. The diagram serves as a critical reference for understanding how processing parameters and friction conditions induce heterogeneous microstructure evolution in the austenitic steel matrix.

This figure presents six sets of laser confocal micrographs showing the microstructure evolution of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density high-strength steel after hot compression under varying temperatures and strain rates. Each set contains three sub-images labeled 1, 2, and 3, representing the difficult deformation zone, small deformation zone, and large deformation zone respectively, illustrating the heterogeneous deformation distribution across the sample section parallel to the loading direction. The six panels correspond to: (a) 1100°C/0.01 s⁻¹, (b) 1100°C/0.1 s⁻¹, (c) 1100°C/10 s⁻¹, (d) 950°C/1 s⁻¹, (e) 1050°C/1 s⁻¹, and (f) 1150°C/1 s⁻¹. The images reveal that dynamic recrystallization (DRX) progresses from partial to complete as deformation increases from zone 1 to zone 3, with significant grain refinement observed in the large deformation zones. At high temperatures (1150°C) and low strain rates (0.01 s⁻¹), the microstructure exhibits coarse equiaxed grains exceeding 70 μm due to full DRX followed by rapid grain growth, whereas at low temperatures (900–950°C) and high strain rates (1–10 s⁻¹), DRX is insufficient with remaining original large grains and poor microstructural homogeneity. All images share a scale bar of 100 μm.

This figure presents the dynamic recrystallization (DRX) average grain size of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density high-strength steel as a function of deformation temperature (900–1150°C) under four different strain rates: 0.01 s⁻¹ (black squares), 0.1 s⁻¹ (red circles), 1 s⁻¹ (blue triangles), and 10 s⁻¹ (magenta stars). The data points represent measurements taken from the core region of compressed samples at 60% height reduction. All curves demonstrate a consistent trend where DRX grain size increases monotonically with decreasing strain rate and increasing temperature. Specifically, at 1150°C and 0.01 s⁻¹, the grain size exceeds 70 μm (approximately 75 μm), indicating rapid grain growth and coarse microstructure formation. Conversely, at lower temperatures (900–950°C) and higher strain rates (1–10 s⁻¹), the DRX grains remain relatively small (approximately 4–22 μm) but incomplete, with retained original coarse grains resulting in poor microstructural homogeneity. The figure experimentally validates the grain size prediction model obtained via Levenberg-Marquardt optimization, confirming that the steel exhibits positive temperature sensitivity and negative strain-rate sensitivity for DRX grain growth, which provides critical guidance for optimizing hot working parameters to achieve fine and uniform grain structures.

The figure presents a three-part validation of a dynamic recrystallization (DRX) grain size prediction model for low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel subjected to hot deformation. Subplot (a) displays experimental grain size versus deformation temperature (900–1150°C) at constant strain rates of 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 10 s⁻¹, demonstrating that grain size increases monotonically with temperature while decreasing with increasing strain rate, with values ranging from approximately 4 μm (at 900°C/10 s⁻¹) to 75 μm (at 1150°C/0.01 s⁻¹). Subplot (b) compares predicted versus experimental grain sizes, showing strong linear correlation with a correlation coefficient R = 0.996 and mean relative error MRE = 4.53%, with data points largely falling within the ±10% deviation bands indicated by red lines. Subplot (c) illustrates the distribution of absolute prediction error versus experimental grain size, confirming that most prediction errors remain below 3 μm as marked by the dashed blue line. These results collectively validate the accuracy and reliability of the established DRX grain size model for predicting microstructural evolution during hot forging of this steel.
